currentSnapshot.definition: Of, relating to, or characteristic of homosexual people, especially men; also used as a self-identifying term within LGBTQ+ communities.
currentSnapshot.usageNote: The dominant contemporary sense. Has also been reclaimed as an identity term by queer communities. A separate derogatory usage (meaning 'lame/bad') emerged in youth slang but is widely considered offensive.
currentSnapshot.exampleUsage: She attended the first Pride parade as a young gay woman.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Full of joy, merry, lighthearted; bright and showy (of colours or dress).
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: The primary and unambiguous sense for centuries. 'A gay song', 'gay colours', 'gay spirits'. Reading pre-20th-century texts, this is almost always the operative meaning.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: The fields were gay with wildflowers and the children danced with gay abandon.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Addicted to pleasures; dissolute, licentious; living a life of pleasure and excess.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: A moral judgment: a 'gay life' could mean a life of promiscuity and excess. A 'gay woman' (17th–19th century) often meant a prostitute. This sense coexisted with the cheerful sense.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: He had lived a gay and reckless life before reform.

historicalSnapshots.definition: Homosexual; used initially as in-group slang within gay communities, emerging into broader usage.
historicalSnapshots.usageNote: The homosexual sense emerged in American slang in the 1920s–30s and became widely known by the 1940s–60s. The older cheerful sense persisted in literature but became increasingly awkward in casual speech.
historicalSnapshots.exampleUsage: In those days, the word 'gay' carried a coded meaning understood within the community.

summaryOfChange.shortSummary: From 'merry and bright' through 'dissolute' to 'homosexual identity'.
summaryOfChange.longSummary: Gay entered English as a bright, cheerful adjective—used freely for centuries in poetry, song, and daily speech to mean joyful, lively, or colourfully dressed. A parallel thread of 'dissolute/licentious' meaning ran from the 17th century. In the early 20th century, American queer communities adopted 'gay' as an in-group identity term, a reclamation that gradually displaced the older senses in everyday speech. By the 1970s the identity sense was dominant, making older literary uses require disambiguation.

keyDates.label: First recorded in English
keyDates.significance: Earliest English use meaning 'joyful, light-hearted', from Old French 'gai'.

keyDates.label: Gertrude Stein's 'Miss Furr and Miss Skeene'
keyDates.significance: One of the earliest literary texts to use 'gay' in its modern sense, though ambiguously.

keyDates.label: Stonewall and gay liberation
keyDates.significance: The Stonewall uprising catalysed gay liberation movements that embraced and popularised 'gay' as an identity term.

keyDates.label: OED adds homosexual sense as primary
keyDates.significance: The OED acknowledged the homosexual sense as the dominant contemporary meaning, marking a lexicographic turning point.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary – gay, adj. (historical senses)
sources.excerpt: Full of or disposed to joy and mirth; light-hearted, exuberantly cheerful, sportive, merry. Also: bright or lively-looking, showy, brilliant.
sources.relevanceNote: Documents the centuries-long primary sense before the semantic shift.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary – gay, adj. (dissolute sense)
sources.excerpt: Addicted to social pleasures and dissipation; of loose or immoral life.
sources.relevanceNote: The 'dissolute' parallel sense shows the moral ambiguity that preceded the modern identity sense.

sources.title: Oxford English Dictionary – gay, adj. (modern identity sense)
sources.excerpt: Homosexual. (Now the dominant sense in general use.)
sources.relevanceNote: OED's acknowledgment of the identity sense as primary.

sources.title: Gay New York: Gender, Urban Culture, and the Making of the Gay Male World
sources.author: George Chauncey
sources.attribution: © Basic Books. Used for educational commentary.
sources.excerpt: The word 'gay' was used by the men themselves as a code word, a way of identifying each other... By the 1940s it had spread beyond the subculture.
sources.relevanceNote: Scholarly history of the term's adoption and spread as an identity word.

sources.title: American Heritage Dictionary – gay
sources.excerpt: Of, relating to, or having a sexual orientation to persons of the same sex.
sources.relevanceNote: Contemporary American lexicographic authority on the dominant modern sense.

relatedWorks.title: Miss Furr and Miss Skeene
relatedWorks.creator: Gertrude Stein
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: One of the earliest literary texts to use 'gay' in a manner that strongly implies the modern sense, making it a key document in the word's semantic history.

relatedWorks.title: The Poems of John Gay
relatedWorks.creator: John Gay
relatedWorks.whyRelevant: The poet John Gay's name illustrates the word's cheerful original meaning — his name was simply an adjective meaning 'merry'.

relatedPages.title: Semantic Drift
relatedPages.summary: The broad phenomenon of word meanings shifting across time.

relatedPages.title: Register and Slang
relatedPages.summary: How in-group language moves into mainstream usage.

relatedConcepts.label: queer
relatedConcepts.note: Underwent a similar reclamation from pejorative to identity term.

relatedConcepts.label: merry
relatedConcepts.note: Shared the 'joyful/cheerful' semantic space with 'gay' before the shift.

ambiguityNotes: Pre-20th-century uses of 'gay' almost always mean 'cheerful/merry' or 'showy'. When reading historical texts, the contemporary sense should not be assumed.
A derogatory slang sense meaning 'lame' or 'bad' emerged in youth culture from the 1970s onward. This usage is widely considered offensive.
The identity sense is the primary contemporary meaning in most English-speaking contexts, but some older speakers still use the cheerful sense unambiguously.

timelineEvents.title: Enters English: 'joyful and bright'
timelineEvents.summary: From Old French 'gai', meaning merry or light-hearted. For centuries this is the uncomplicated meaning.

timelineEvents.title: The dissolute parallel meaning
timelineEvents.summary: A moralistic strand: 'gay' comes to describe those addicted to pleasure, a 'gay woman' meaning a woman of loose morals.

timelineEvents.title: Gertrude Stein's encoded use
timelineEvents.summary: Stein's story 'Miss Furr and Miss Skeene' uses 'gay' in a way that implied the emerging identity sense to in-group readers.

timelineEvents.title: Stonewall and identity politics
timelineEvents.summary: The Stonewall uprising and subsequent liberation movements transformed 'gay' into a proud, public identity term, displacing the older cheerful sense in casual speech.
